Fractal Art: Why Fractals Are Beautiful, And Why They Are Sometimes Art, Sometimes Design.
==========================================================================================

By [Chris Melchior.](https://unconstrainedtime.com/2024/02/fractal-art-why-fractals-are-beautiful-and-why-they-are-sometimes-art-sometimes-design/#Chris-Melchior-author-block)

**Introduction**
----------------

After looking at the basics of what fractals are, some types of them, and some examples of their uses, I then explore beauty and aesthetics and how they relate to fractals and both have a little-known relationship with well-being.

I then go into detail about the differences between art and design, and how they apply to what people call “fractal art”, then looking at lots of examples, finishing with our own unique fractal creation.

**Fractal Basics**
------------------

What is a fractal?

A fractal is a complex geometric shape or pattern that displays self-similarity at different scales, meaning it exhibits similar structures or patterns regardless of the level of magnification. These intricate and often infinitely detailed formations can be generated through mathematical equations and are prevalent in nature, art, and computer-generated graphics.

Fractals encapsulate the concept of chaos and order coexisting, offering a fascinating lens through which to explore the inherent complexity and beauty found in both mathematical abstraction and the natural world.

**Types Of Fractals**

Fractals, intricate and self-replicating geometric patterns, manifest in diverse forms across mathematics, nature, and art.

![Image 3: Mandelbrot set fractal](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Mandelbrot-set-1-246x300.jpg)
The earliest discovered type is the Mandelbrot set (above), characterized by endlessly complex patterns emerging from simple equations.

![Image 4: Julia-set fractal](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Julia-set-300x239.png)

“Julia 3” By Pokey’s Dad. [Details and license here.](https://flickr.com/photos/dcardani/3265403911/)

The Julia set (above), another iterated fractal, closely relates to the Mandelbrot set, and displays unique shapes based on varying parameters. Fractals extend beyond mathematics, infiltrating natural phenomena like coastlines, mountains, and clouds.

![Image 5: koch snowflake](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Koch-snowflake-300x282.png)
The Koch snowflake (above) exemplifies a geometric fractal with infinite self-replicating triangles, while the Sierpinski triangle recursively removes triangles to create a striking pattern. Fractal geometry finds applications in computer graphics, generating realistic landscapes and textures.

![Image 6: L-system fractal Tree](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/L-system_Tree04-300x295.jpg)

“L-system Tree04” By Nevit Dilmen. [Details and license here.](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:L-system_Tree04.jpg)

L-system fractals (above) produce branching structures, mirroring the growth of plants.

Fractals of many different types illuminate the interconnectedness between mathematics and the natural world, providing a captivating glimpse into the complexity inherent in structures that have a simple basis (fractal algorithms are typically just a few lines of code).

**Fractals Used In Science And Technology**

Fractals play a pivotal role in science and technology, serving as powerful tools across various disciplines. In medicine, fractal analysis aids in studying the intricate and irregular patterns of biological structures, such as blood vessels and cardiac rhythms, offering insights into physiological processes. Fractal-based algorithms enhance image compression and data analysis, enabling more efficient storage and retrieval of information.

In telecommunications, fractals are employed in the design of signal processing, optimizing communication systems for increased efficiency and signal strength. The self-replicating nature of fractals finds applications in creating efficient and compact antennas for wireless devices, enhancing connectivity.

![Image 7: storm from above earth](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/cold-front-warm-front-hurricane-felix-76969-300x205.jpeg)
Furthermore, fractals are instrumental in the simulation and modelling of natural phenomena, from weather patterns to the growth of plants. In climate science, fractal geometry helps scientists analyse and predict complex weather systems. Additionally, they find utility in the oil and gas industry for the simulation of porous rock structures, aiding in resource exploration and extraction, as well as in predicting the way prices of stocks and shares change over time.

In technology, fractals contribute to the generation of realistic computer graphics, enabling the creation of lifelike landscapes and textures. They are also utilized in encryption algorithms and data security, enhancing protection.

Overall, the application of fractals in science and technology underscores their versatility and significance in understanding, simulating, and optimizing complex systems across various domains.

**Why Are Fractals Beautiful?**
-------------------------------

First, why is anything beautiful or ugly?

**What Is Beauty?**

“_The most beautiful thing we can experience is the mysterious. It is the source of all true art and science._” Albert Einstein. Fractals have a sort-of “alien” quality to them. They are often intuitively natural and yet have a somehow otherworldly mystery to them.

When I say “beauty”, I am talking here about aesthetics applying to objects in general, rather than just which humans are more attractive to other humans.

![Image 8: Monet water lilies - fractal marks](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Monet-water-lilies-300x242.jpg)

“Claude Monet” By G. Starke. [Details and license here.](https://flickr.com/photos/157267975@N08/41417967575/in/photolist-266XUMe-25TvVVR-27tAt6x-2nBdQ9z-2nBdQ9K-2nBdQ9Q-pgQuf-p9gacM-7FyFDi-p9uMLd-422tpu-2nBcHo6-2ny8VAc-p9uMES-TZf9ig-2oGcTV4-2g9WTGt-25TwG3h-vmPLvP-d7qbiy-5M874f-2nB6fQM-47zFFA-2nBcHvf-7rK1HU-2nBbjad-2nBcHjd-QH56Uj-mLrgx-WZL5Mp-xZ5Lg-2nB6fK6-2nBcHiG-2nBbjde-2nBcHtB-oS3uff-2nB6fTY-zekpF-2nB6fTn-2nBdQ5X-2nB6fRJ-2nB6fTN-2nBcHpZ-2nBdQ3C-2nBbf2G-2nBdQ56-8a4TFE-vfviRw-2CbUR-2nBdPVP)

Beauty and ugliness are partially subjective concepts deeply rooted in individual perspectives, cultural influences, and societal norms. Beauty often encompasses qualities admired for their harmony, symmetry, balance, colour, complexity, and which transcend physical appearances. It resonates as a positive, uplifting force that captivates and inspires.

On the contrary, ugliness is often linked to perceived imperfections, disharmony, or deviations from conventional standards. However, the notion of ugliness is also partially subjective, as it may derive to a significant extent from societal biases and cultural conditioning. What one culture deems as beautiful, another may label as unattractive. These also evolve over time, such as the change in preference in Western societies from women with a larger figure, to more recent preferences for a slimmer figure, which is itself now being challenged conceptually by some areas of society.

Both beauty and ugliness coexist in a delicate interplay, reflecting the diversity of human experiences. Appreciating the contrast between them allows for a richer understanding of aesthetics and the intricacies of human perception. Ultimately, the boundaries between beauty and ugliness are, to some extent fluid, shaped by personal perspectives, cultural contexts, and the ever-evolving nature of societal ideals, while also having a universal objective component.

**Why Fractals Are Beautiful**

As well as harmony, symmetry, balance, colour, complexity, “fractality” is known to be a component of beauty.

Our brain loves patterns. We have a well developed ability of pattern recognition. And fractals are a culmination of a pattern within a pattern within a pattern.

![Image 9: Leaf showing fractal pattern](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/leaf-1637974_1280-300x216.jpg)
People tend to like things which are familiar, and most of the natural world is made of fractals. Similarly, people tend to find attractive things which are similar to themselves, and human bodies, as part of the natural world, have lots of fractal properties.

Fractals captivate the human eye and mind with their innate beauty, transcending traditional notions of symmetry and the usual view of simplicity. Their charm lies in their combination of simplicity and complexity, on the fascinating edge between order and chaos. All art can be analysed in terms of setting up a repetitive pattern and then breaking it. Too much repetition is boring, but too much chaos can be confusing and difficult to relate to. Fractals bridge that polarity, having ever-changing yet intuitively acceptable patterns based on relationships which are similar but not identical.

The delicate balance of repeating patterns and unpredictability creates a visual harmony that resonates with our innate sense of aesthetic appreciation. Fractals echo the complexity found in nature, from the branching of trees to the irregularities of coastlines, forging a profound connection between mathematical abstraction and the organic world. In art, science, and technology, fractals inspire creativity, providing a visual language to express the beauty inherent in complexity based on simple principles, offering a unique window into the wondrous intricacies of our universe.

**How Beauty And Fractals Relate To Well-Being**

Why do fractals promote positive mental health? . . . Because most of nature is made of them, it has been suggested that our brains are actually evolved to positively respond to them. Viewing fractal patterns has been shown to alter the way that the brain functions and the patterns of brain waves that are produced, resulting in a reduction in stress of 60%.

![Image 10: flower floating in hands - fractals and well-being](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/flower-water-handsjpg-254x300.jpg)
Aesthetics, the study of beauty and sensory experiences, is intricately tied to well-being. Environments, objects, and experiences that are aesthetically pleasing have a positive impact on mental and emotional states. Beautiful surroundings, whether in nature or art, can reduce stress, elevate mood, and enhance overall happiness.

Incorporating fractals in architectural designs can mitigate visual strain and discomfort caused by the unnatural straight lines of Euclidean spaces and reduce stress, resonating with the biophilic idea of humans’ innate connection to nature. For example, patients recover more rapidly from surgery if their room has a window looking out on nature.

Aesthetic preferences vary, but the pursuit and appreciation of beauty contribute to a sense of fulfilment and connection. Personalized aesthetics empower individuals to create spaces that resonate with their identity, fostering a positive atmosphere and promoting a holistic sense of well-being by nurturing the harmony between one’s surroundings and inner emotional landscape.

A fascinating study: “[Fractals: the hidden beauty and potential therapeutic effect of the natural world](fractals: the hidden beauty and potential therapeutic effect of the natural world)” goes into detail about one theory of how fractals affect one’s state of mind, specifically the relevance of a fractal pattern’s visual complexity.

This may be related to the fact that aesthetics to some degree aligns with functionality. For example . . . I remember hearing the expert presenter at an air show saying that if an airplane looks beautiful then it will probably fly well.

![Image 11: Spitfire flying - an example of beauty denoting functionality](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/spitfire-1252722_1280-300x201.jpg)
**What’s the difference between art and design?**
-------------------------------------------------

Fractal art, like photography, was greeted by scepticism initially, then later became an accepted part of the art world. Here is what is believed to be the earliest surviving camera photograph, taken circa 1826:

![Image 12: the earliest surviving camera photograph](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/View_from_the_Window_at_Le_Gras_colorized_2020_new-300x123.jpg)

“View from the Window at Le Gras colorized 2020 new” By Jonnychiwa. [Details and license here.](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:View_from_the_Window_at_Le_Gras_colorized_2020_new.png)

Art and design, while intertwined, serve distinct purposes and embody different processes. Art is a form of creative expression driven by individual inspiration, often emphasizing emotion, conceptual ideas, and personal interpretation:

![Image 13: Van Gogh Starry Night paintiing](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/Van_Gogh_-_Starry_Night_-_Google_Art_Project-300x237.jpg)
Conversely, design is a purposeful and intentional process aimed at solving problems or fulfilling specific objectives. It involves creating functional and aesthetically pleasing solutions within defined constraints, often driven by user needs, functionality, and efficiency. Design is inherently practical, considering usability and the communication of a specific message or functionality.

There are broadly agreed characteristics that define art:

*   Art is enjoyed for its own sake, not for it’s practical usefulness.
*   Art is focused more on aesthetics than on functionality.
*   Art goes deeper than decoration (Decorative arts are defined as design)
*   Art has special focus
*   Art is unique
*   Art is creativity relating to conceptual ideas used as a means to communicate, evoke emotions and present challenging perspectives and concepts.
*   Art is personal . . both in terms of being personal to the individual who created it, and in terms of who made it matter to the viewer.

Like beauty, these characteristics have some degree of subjectivity. For a lot more details on each one of these points, see my article “[Are Watches Art](https://unconstrainedtime.com/2024/01/are-watches-art-are-there-works-of-art-which-also-tell-the-time/)?”

What people call “fractal art” is almost always things that are enjoyed for their own sake, focusing on aesthetics, have a special focus, and are unique. So to distinguish art from design, the main areas to consider are:

Is it decorative or intrinsic? There are plenty of people adding a fractal image to the face of an otherwise conventional watch or other object, but that’s a lot more decorative than intrinsic and thus is design rather than art. The same is true of the results of a lot of collaborations between watchmakers and artists, with the art typically being a decorative addition to the dial of an otherwise clearly design-based watch.

Is it a personal exploration that conveys some fascinating concept or emotion? Artists create a series of artworks, exploring and developing their own personal style, and have a deep interest in what they are conveying or evoking. If you look at a fractal image and can recognize which individual created it, then that is at least some way towards being art rather than design, unlike the more generic fractal posters which could have been made by anyone, in the same way that it doesn’t matter who made a design that functions well . . . if someone else had made something which functions similarly, that would be equally useful.

![Image 14: Botticelli the birth of Venus painting](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/painting-la-nascita-di-venere-botticelli-the-birth-of-venus-preview-300x192.jpg)
As usual, rather than just telling you my opinion, I have introduced you to the understanding enabling you to decide for yourself what is art and what is not. So you can now think for yourself . . . don’t just believe what others tell you on the subject.

**Examples Of Fractal Art/Design**
----------------------------------

Most people are familiar with beautiful 2D fractal images, such as these, the 2nd and 3rd being my own creations:

![Image 15: fractal](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/fractal-art-1-188x300.jpeg)![Image 16: Fractal art - processed flame fractal](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Flame-fractal-10a-300x300.jpg)

![Image 17: Flame fractal - fractal art](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/fractal-1280071_1280-300x300.jpg)![Image 18: Fractal art - processed heart-shaped flame fractal](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Fractal-heart-9x6-1-300x255.jpg)

And more recently, there has been an explosion of beautiful 3D fractal images such as these:

![Image 19: Fractal art - 3D fractal Manedlbulb](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Power_16_mandelbulb_fractal_detail_of_the_north_pole-300x222.jpg)

“Power 16 mandelbulb fractal detail of the north pole” By Ondřej Karlík. [Details and license here.](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Power_16_mandelbulb_fractal_detail_of_the_north_pole.jpg)

![Image 20: Fractal art - 3D fractal](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/underwater-nature-sea-sponge-invertebrate-preview-300x225.jpg)![Image 21: Fractal landscape](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/planet_discover_fantasy_world_voyager_virtual_landscape_model_sience_fiction_fractals-481870-300x177.jpg)

![Image 22: Fractal art - 3D fractal](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/800px-Frame_0003404_28052019-300x169.jpg)

“Frame 0003404 28052019” By PantheraLeo1359531. [Details and license here.](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Frame_0003404_28052019.jpg)

![Image 23: Fractal art - 3D fractal](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/arches-662992_1280-300x225.jpg)![Image 24: Fractal art - 3D fractal](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/fractal-1118515_1280-300x225.jpg)

**Art Which Uses Fractals**

Most artworks have some degree of fractal nature. Marks made by a brush are usually fractal, with the fractal nature of the marks being used as a significant part of the focus for artists like Monet and J. M. W. Turner as well as in many examples of Asian brush calligraphy, especially the larger pieces.

![Image 25: Howard Hodgkin painting - fractal marks](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Howard-Hodgkin-300x201.jpg)

“Howard Hodgkin at the Hepworth -12” By alh1. [Details and license here.](https://flickr.com/photos/allan_harris/37525824676/)

Many abstract artists use obviously fractal marks, such as Franz Kline (below), Howard Hodgkin (above) and Mark Rothko.

![Image 26: Franz Kline black and white abstract - fractal marks](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Franz-Kline-300x242.jpg)

“Franz Kline – Mahoning, 1956” By G. Starke. [Details and license here.](https://flickr.com/photos/157267975@N08/40774607660/)

Since the natural world is composed mostly of fractals, most landscape painters could be seen as fractal artists. Painters have long been drawn to the allure of natural landscapes, using their brushes to convey the beauty and serenity of the outdoors. One such luminary is Thomas Cole, a key figure in the Hudson River School, known for his sublime depictions of the American wilderness in works like “The Oxbow.” (below).

![Image 27: Thomas Cole The Oxbow - painting of a fractal landscape](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Cole_Thomas_The_Oxbow_The_Connecticut_River_near_Northampton_1836-300x204.jpg)

(public domain image)

The Dutch painter Jacob van Ruisdael, active in the 17th century, captured the pastoral beauty of landscapes with meticulous detail, influencing generations of landscape artists with his nuanced compositions (below).

![Image 28: Jacob van Ruisdael - Peasant cottages in the dunes - painting of a fractal landscape](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Jacob_van_Ruisdael_-_Peasant_cottages_in_the_dunes-300x233.jpg)

(public domain image)

![Image 29: John Constable Stratford Mill - painting of a fractal landscape](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/john-constable-84952_1280-300x206.jpg)

(public domain image)

In the 19th century, the prolific John Constable depicted the English countryside with a keen eye for detail and a deep connection to the rural landscape. His masterpiece, “The Hay Wain,” exemplifies his ability to infuse scenes with a sense of tranquillity and natural beauty. Another great example is his “Stratford Mill” (above).

![Image 30: Katsushika Hokusai The Great Wave off Kanagawa](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/12/800px-Katsushika_Hokusai_The_Great_Wave_off_Kanagawa_1830-300x204.jpg)

(public domain image)

Other examples of fractals in art include da Vinci’s Turbulence (1500), Hokusai’s Great Wave (1830, above), and M.C. Escher’s Circle Series from the1950s. One of the best known examples of fractal expressionism is found in Jackson Pollock’s dripped patterns (below). It is also interesting to note that fake Pollock paintings can be distinguished from genuine ones by the different properties of the fractals they use.

![Image 31: Jackson- Pollock drip painting - an example of fractal art](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/Jackson-Pollock-300x198.jpg)

“Jackson Pollock at MoMA” By edwardhblake. [Details and license here.](https://flickr.com/photos/eblake/14668871533/in/photolist-omeNsa-aB4P9o-5i38y8-PDJyg-5i7t8A-a8PKC3-9rvQYH-5g5okf-amkkSM-dvqGud-hgfFy-8Pp4Zx-ijzXJG-amnRbQ-68ebF2-dhn95X-4quxqJ-7yX8Cz-GMCZ86-a8LJap-amnWBG-4spmKr-pnvsz-4spn6M-d7h4s-4stpLL-amkoiZ-dvqJoN-CXLy2n-SfarJr-jdyRN-dvqGZs-dvqTaw-4spmW4-4spng8-NqpbRD-JFgeaR-6xYWY3-4spn9T-8JUwhf-4stqjh-2jgRYSi-9aXDZs-24yy1rC-PMx4HN-CXFNZz-2aMVUqF-qtoqPt-24yxZPL-C1N8fb)

Yves Klein, a French artist associated with the Nouveau Réalisme movement, used fractals indirectly in his artwork. His exploration of the infinite and the void, as seen in his iconic blue monochromes, resonates with the concept of infinity found in fractals. Klein’s artistic philosophy embraced the sublime and the boundless, echoing the endless complexity inherent in fractal patterns:

![Image 32: Yves Klein blue artwork](https://unconstrainedtime.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/02/495px-Laccord_bleu_RE_10_1960_cropped-248x300.jpg)

“L’accord bleu (RE 10), 1960 cropped” By Jaredzimmerman (WMF). [Details a](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:L%27accord_bleu_(RE_10),_1960_cropped.jpg)[nd license here.](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:L%27accord_bleu_(RE_10),_1960_cropped.jpg)

Digital artist Scott Draves created the Electric Sheep, a collaborative and evolving screensaver that generates abstract fractal animations. Users contribute to the evolving “flock” by voting on their favourite animations, influencing the future iterations. Draves’ work demonstrates the collaborative and generative potential of fractals in the digital realm, allowing for an ever-expanding and diverse visual landscape.

British artist William Latham collaborated with mathematician Stephen Todd and computer scientist Peter Todd to create “The Organic Art of William Latham,” a series of computer-generated images that explore the possibilities of genetic algorithms and fractal geometry. These evolving and organic forms demonstrate the fusion of art, mathematics, and technology.

American digital artist Vicky Brago-Mitchell incorporates fractals into her work, exploring the interplay between chaos and order. Her pieces, often created using fractal-generating software, showcase intricate patterns and vivid colours, highlighting the captivating visual appeal of fractals in the digital art space.

Japanese artist Makoto Nakamura blends traditional Japanese art techniques with fractal geometry to create mesmerizing paintings.

Fractal art pioneer Janet Parke has been creating intricate fractal-based works since the 1980s. Her pieces often feature vibrant colours, intricate details, and a sense of cosmic wonder. Parke’s exploration of fractals has significantly contributed to the development and recognition of fractal art as a unique genre.

**Photography Of Fractal Subjects**

Nature is mostly fractals. So any artist who photographs nature is, in essence, doing a form of fractal art.

Photographers who capture the essence of trees often create compelling visual narratives that celebrate the beauty, majesty, and symbolic significance of these arboreal wonders. One such luminary is Ansel Adams, renowned for his black-and-white landscapes, including iconic images of towering trees in the American West like the “Jeffrey Pine, Sentinel Dome.” Many of his famous images which immortalize the grandeur of the American West, include obviously fractal subjects (like trees and mountain ranges), such as “Adams: The Tetons and the Snake River” below . . .

![Image 2: A fern repeats its pattern at various scales.](https://th-thumbnailer.cdn-si-edu.com/zz2vu8S9JXVawoXykIEM8ILMujY=/1026x668/https://tf-cmsv2-smithsonianmag-media.s3.amazonaws.com/filer/3b/1f/3b1f766a-d22a-4937-b099-0e96fe62b1ee/qxmjn3t6-1490887263.jpg)

 A fern repeats its pattern at various scales. Michael , CC BY-NC

Humans are visual creatures. Objects we call “beautiful” or “aesthetic” are a crucial part of our humanity. Even the oldest known examples of[rock and cave art served aesthetic](https://www.amazon.com/Aesthetics-Rock-Art-Thomas-Heyd/dp/075463924X?xid=PS_smithsonian)rather than utilitarian roles. Although aesthetics is often regarded as an ill-defined vague quality,[research groups like mine](https://blogs.uoregon.edu/richardtaylor/?xid=PS_smithsonian)are using sophisticated techniques to quantify it – and its impact on the observer.

![Image 3](https://pl.primis.tech/live/liveView.php?)![Image 4](https://pl.primis.tech/live/liveView.php?)

We’re finding that aesthetic images can induce staggering changes to the body, including[radical reductions in the observer’s stress levels](http://doi.org/10.1162/leon.2006.39.3.245?xid=PS_smithsonian). Job stress alone is estimated to cost American businesses[many billions of dollars annually](http://www.businessnewsdaily.com/2267-workplace-stress-health-epidemic-perventable-employee-assistance-programs.html?xid=PS_smithsonian), so studying aesthetics holds a huge potential benefit to society.

Researchers are untangling just what makes particular works of art or natural scenes visually appealing and stress-relieving – and one crucial factor is the presence of the repetitive patterns called fractals.

![Image 5: Are fractals the key to why Pollock’s work captivates?](https://th-thumbnailer.cdn-si-edu.com/ZDFCkQ8dqOTq7vjEWGXa0n35h7g=/fit-in/1072x0/https://tf-cmsv2-smithsonianmag-media.s3.amazonaws.com/filer/fb/b0/fbb09cd3-000a-422b-b7e7-f5603c6b039d/image-20170330-4592-167yg1n.jpg)

 Are fractals the key to why Pollock’s work captivates? AP Photo/LM Otero

Pleasing patterns, in art and in nature
---------------------------------------

When it comes to aesthetics, who better to study than famous artists? They are, after all, the visual experts. My research group took this approach with[Jackson Pollock](https://www.moma.org/artists/4675?xid=PS_smithsonian), who rose to the peak of modern art in the late 1940s by pouring paint directly from a can onto horizontal canvases laid across his studio floor. Although battles raged among Pollock scholars regarding the meaning of his splattered patterns, many agreed they had an organic, natural feel to them.

My scientific curiosity was stirred when I learned that[many of nature’s objects are fractal](http://us.macmillan.com/thefractalgeometryofnature/benoitbmandelbrot/9780716711865?xid=PS_smithsonian), featuring patterns that repeat at increasingly fine magnifications. For example, think of a tree. First you see the big branches growing out of the trunk. Then you see smaller versions growing out of each big branch. As you keep zooming in, finer and finer branches appear, all the way down to the smallest twigs. Other examples of nature’s fractals include clouds, rivers, coastlines and mountains.

In 1999, my group used computer pattern analysis techniques to show that[Pollock’s paintings are as fractal](https://blogs.uoregon.edu/richardtaylor/files/2015/12/PollockScientificAmerican-2ees1wh.pdf?xid=PS_smithsonian)as patterns found in natural scenery. Since then, more than 10[different groups](https://blogs.uoregon.edu/richardtaylor/2016/02/08/fractal-analysis-of-jackson-pollocks-poured-paintings/?xid=PS_smithsonian)have performed[various forms of fractal analysis](https://blogs.uoregon.edu/richardtaylor/2017/01/04/the-facts-about-pollocks-fractals/?xid=PS_smithsonian)on his paintings. Pollock’s ability to express nature’s fractal aesthetics helps explain the enduring popularity of his work.

The impact of nature’s aesthetics is surprisingly powerful. In the 1980s, architects found that patients recovered more quickly from surgery when given[hospital rooms with windows looking out on nature](http://dx.doi.org/10.1126/science.6143402?xid=PS_smithsonian). Other studies since then have demonstrated that just looking at pictures of natural scenes can change the way a person’s autonomic nervous system[responds to stress](http://dx.doi.org/10.1021/es305019p?xid=PS_smithsonian).

![Image 6: Are fractals the secret to some soothing natural scenes?](https://th-thumbnailer.cdn-si-edu.com/CnU08t2tDuWVJov86n4u4osHA3g=/fit-in/1072x0/https://tf-cmsv2-smithsonianmag-media.s3.amazonaws.com/filer/89/f5/89f55c24-ab44-44db-9b9f-88d8ddf15451/image-20170330-4557-1tpeqb6.jpg)

 Are fractals the secret to some soothing natural scenes? Ronan, CC BY-NC-ND

For me, this raises the same question I’d asked of Pollock: Are fractals responsible? Collaborating with psychologists and neuroscientists,[we measured people’s responses to fractals](https://blogs.uoregon.edu/richardtaylor/2016/02/03/human-physiological-responses-to-fractals-in-nature-and-art/?xid=PS_smithsonian)found in nature (using photos of natural scenes), art (Pollock’s paintings) and mathematics (computer generated images) and discovered a universal effect we labeled “[fractal fluency](http://doi.org/10.1007/978-1-4939-3995-4_30?xid=PS_smithsonian).”

Through exposure to nature’s fractal scenery, people’s visual systems have adapted to efficiently process fractals with ease. We found that this adaptation occurs at many stages of the visual system, from the way our eyes move to which regions of the brain get activated. This fluency puts us in a comfort zone and so we enjoy looking at fractals. Crucially,[we used EEG](https://www.smithsonianmag.com/innovation/fractal-patterns-nature-and-art-are-aesthetically-pleasing-and-stress-reducing-180962738/doi.org/10.1068/p5918?xid=PS_smithsonian)to record the brain’s electrical activity and[skin conductance techniques](http://doi.org/10.1162/leon.2006.39.3.245?xid=PS_smithsonian)to show that this aesthetic experience is accompanied by stress reduction of 60 percent – a surprisingly large effect for a nonmedicinal treatment. This physiological change even accelerates[post-surgical recovery rates](http://dx.doi.org/10.1126/science.6143402?xid=PS_smithsonian).

Artists intuit the appeal of fractals
-------------------------------------

It’s therefore not surprising to learn that, as visual experts, artists have been embedding fractal patterns in their works through the centuries and across many cultures. Fractals can be found, for example, in Roman, Egyptian, Aztec, Incan and Mayan works. My favorite examples of fractal art from more recent times include[da Vinci’s Turbulence](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Etudes_turbulences_-_L%C3%A9onard_de_Vinci.jpg?xid=PS_smithsonian)(1500),[Hokusai’s Great Wave](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Great_Wave_off_Kanagawa2.jpg?xid=PS_smithsonian)(1830),[M.C. Escher’s Circle Series](http://mathstat.slu.edu/escher/index.php/Escher's_Circle_Limit_Exploration?xid=PS_smithsonian)(1950s) and, of course,[Pollock’s poured paintings](http://www.metmuseum.org/art/collection/search/488978?sortBy=Relevance&ft=Pollock&offset=0&rpp=20&pos=3?xid=PS_smithsonian).

Although prevalent in art, the fractal repetition of patterns represents an artistic challenge. For instance, many people have attempted to fake Pollock’s fractals and failed. Indeed, our fractal analysis has[helped identify fake Pollocks](http://doi.org/10.1038/439648a?xid=PS_smithsonian)in high-profile cases. Recent studies by others show that fractal analysis can[help distinguish real from fake Pollocks](http://doi.org/10.1504/IJART.2015.067389?xid=PS_smithsonian)with a 93 percent success rate.

How artists create their fractals fuels the nature-versus-nurture debate in art: To what extent is aesthetics determined by automatic unconscious mechanisms inherent in the artist’s biology, as opposed to their intellectual and cultural concerns? In Pollock’s case, his fractal aesthetics resulted from an intriguing mixture of both. His fractal patterns originated from his body motions (specifically an[automatic process related to balance](http://doi.org/10.1007/s00221-008-1521-7?xid=PS_smithsonian)known to be fractal). But he spent 10 years consciously refining his pouring technique to increase the visual complexity of these fractal patterns.

![Image 7: The Rorschach inkblot test relies on what you read in to the image.](https://th-thumbnailer.cdn-si-edu.com/eAtIwhCw67uExoDcefdaVMXcQW0=/fit-in/1072x0/https://tf-cmsv2-smithsonianmag-media.s3.amazonaws.com/filer/ee/67/ee67286f-2e7c-4328-b3dd-41f3e5b605c2/image-20170330-4557-1kk2sdc.jpg)

 The Rorschach inkblot test relies on what you read in to the image. Hermann Rorschach

Fractal complexity
------------------

Pollock’s motivation for continually increasing the complexity of his fractal patterns became apparent recently when I studied the[fractal properties of Rorschach inkblots](http://doi.org/10.1038/nature.2017.21473?xid=PS_smithsonian). These abstract blots are famous because people see imaginary forms (figures and animals) in them. I explained this process in terms of the fractal fluency effect, which enhances people’s pattern recognition processes. The low complexity fractal inkblots made this process trigger-happy, fooling observers into seeing images that aren’t there.

Pollock disliked the idea that viewers of his paintings were distracted by such imaginary figures, which he called “extra cargo.” He intuitively increased the complexity of his works to prevent this phenomenon.

Pollock’s abstract expressionist colleague,[Willem De Kooning](https://www.moma.org/artists/3213?xid=PS_smithsonian), also painted fractals. When he was diagnosed with dementia, some art scholars called for his retirement amid concerns that that it would reduce the nurture component of his work. Yet, although they predicted a deterioration in his paintings, his[later works](http://www.sfgate.com/entertainment/article/APEX-OR-DECLINE-The-great-painter-Willem-de-3022569.php?xid=PS_smithsonian)[conveyed a peacefulness](http://www.dekooning.org/the-artist/biography?xid=PS_smithsonian)missing from his earlier pieces. Recently, the fractal complexity of his paintings was shown to[drop steadily as he slipped into dementia](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2016/12/161229113520.htm?xid=PS_smithsonian). The study focused on seven artists with different neurological conditions and highlighted the potential of using art works as a new tool for studying these diseases. To me, the most inspiring message is that, when fighting these diseases, artists can still create beautiful artworks.

![Image 8: Recognizing how looking at fractals](https://th-thumbnailer.cdn-si-edu.com/tc_3zzkoMKqN4aVC6jFfG96EKhU=/fit-in/1072x0/https://tf-cmsv2-smithsonianmag-media.s3.amazonaws.com/filer/90/a7/90a7f1a6-7da4-4102-aea4-3bedfe2497d4/image-20170330-4576-1gise5y.jpg)

 Recognizing how looking at fractals reduces stress means it’s possible to create retinal implants that mimic the mechanism. Nautilus image via www.shutterstock.com

My main research focuses on[developing retinal implants to restore vision](https://around.uoregon.edu/content/uo-idea-bio-inspired-implant-wins-900000-grant?xid=PS_smithsonian)to victims of retinal diseases. At first glance, this goal seems a long way from Pollock’s art. Yet, it was his work that gave me the first clue to fractal fluency and the role nature’s fractals can play in keeping people’s stress levels in check. To[make sure my bio-inspired implants induce the same stress reduction](http://www.iop.org/news/11/april/page_50684.html?xid=PS_smithsonian)when looking at nature’s fractals as normal eyes do, they closely mimic the retina’s design.

When I started my Pollock research, I never imagined it would inform artificial eye designs. This, though, is the power of interdisciplinary endeavors – thinking “out of the box” leads to unexpected but potentially revolutionary ideas.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Form of algorithmic art

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Hindu_Temple_Design.jpg/250px-Hindu_Temple_Design.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Hindu_Temple_Design.jpg)

[Hindu temples](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hindu_temple_architecture "Hindu temple architecture") feature self-similar, fractal-like structures, where parts resemble the whole.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-1)

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Selimiye_Mosque%2C_Dome.jpg/250px-Selimiye_Mosque%2C_Dome.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Selimiye_Mosque,_Dome.jpg)

[Islamic geometric patterns](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Islamic_geometric_patterns "Islamic geometric patterns") are reminiscent of fractal art, as on the main dome of [Selimiye Mosque](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selimiye_Mosque_(Edirne) "Selimiye Mosque (Edirne)") in [Edirne](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edirne "Edirne"), Turkey, with [self-similar](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Self-similar "Self-similar") patterns.

**Fractal art** is a form of [algorithmic art](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algorithmic_art "Algorithmic art") created by calculating [fractal](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal "Fractal") objects and representing the calculation results as still digital images, animations, and [media](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Algorithmic_composition "Algorithmic composition"). Fractal art developed from the mid-1980s onwards.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-fgad-2) It is a genre of [computer art](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_art "Computer art") and [digital art](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_art "Digital art") which are part of [new media art](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/New_media_art "New media art"). The [mathematical beauty](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematical_beauty "Mathematical beauty") of fractals lies at the intersection of [generative art](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Generative_art "Generative art") and [computer art](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Computer_art "Computer art"). They combine to produce a type of [abstract art](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Abstract_art "Abstract art").

Fractal art (especially in the western world) is rarely drawn or painted by hand. It is usually created indirectly with the assistance of [fractal-generating software](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal-generating_software "Fractal-generating software"), iterating through three phases: setting parameters of appropriate fractal software; executing the possibly lengthy calculation; and evaluating the product. In some cases, other [graphics programs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graphics_software "Graphics software") are used to further modify the images produced. This is called post-processing. Non-fractal imagery may also be integrated into the artwork.[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-mra-3) The [Julia set](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Julia_set "Julia set") and [Mandelbrot sets](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mandelbrot_set "Mandelbrot set") can be considered as [icons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Icon "Icon") of fractal art.[[4]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-hema-4)

It was assumed that fractal art could not have developed without computers because of the [calculative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calculation "Calculation") capabilities they provide.[[5]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-dima-5) Fractals are generated by applying [iterative](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iteration "Iteration") methods to solving non-linear equations or [polynomial equations](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polynomial_equation "Polynomial equation"). Fractals are any of various extremely irregular curves or shapes for which any suitably chosen part is similar in shape to a given larger or smaller part when magnified or reduced to the same size.[[6]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-6)

Types
-----

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fractal_art&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Types")]

[![Image 6](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/NonIntegerMultibrot_-_Breaking_of_Space.jpg/250px-NonIntegerMultibrot_-_Breaking_of_Space.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:NonIntegerMultibrot_-_Breaking_of_Space.jpg)

A detail from a non-integer [Multibrot set](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Multibrot_set "Multibrot set")

There are many different kinds of fractal images. They can be subdivided into several groups.

*   Fractals derived from standard geometry by using iterative transformations on an initial common figure like a straight line (the Cantor dust or the [von Koch curve](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koch_snowflake "Koch snowflake")), a triangle (the [Sierpinski triangle](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sierpinski_triangle "Sierpinski triangle")), or a cube (the [Menger sponge](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Menger_sponge "Menger sponge")). The first fractal figures invented near the end of the 19th and early 20th centuries belong to this group.
*   IFS ([iterated function systems](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iterated_function_systems "Iterated function systems"))
*   [Strange attractors](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Strange_attractors "Strange attractors")
*   [Fractal flame](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Flame_fractals "Flame fractals")
*   [L-system](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/L-system "L-system") fractals
*   Fractals created by the iteration of complex [polynomials](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polynomial "Polynomial").
*   [Newton fractals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Newton_fractal "Newton fractal"), including [Nova fractals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nova_fractal "Nova fractal")
*   Fractals generated over [quaternions](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quaternion "Quaternion") and other [Cayley-Dickson algebras](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cayley-Dickson_algebra "Cayley-Dickson algebra")[[7]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-7)
*   [Fractal terrains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_terrain "Fractal terrain") generated by random fractal processes[[8]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-8)
*   [Mandelbulbs](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mandelbulb "Mandelbulb") are a form of three dimensional fractal.

[Fractal Expressionism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_Expressionism "Fractal Expressionism") is a term used to differentiate traditional visual art that incorporates fractal elements such as [self-similarity](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Self-similarity "Self-similarity") for example. Perhaps the best example of fractal expressionism is found in [Jackson Pollock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jackson_Pollock "Jackson Pollock")'s dripped patterns. They have been analysed and found to contain a [fractal dimension](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_dimension "Fractal dimension") which has been attributed to his technique.[[9]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-coex-9)

Techniques
----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fractal_art&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Techniques")]

Main article: [Fractal-generating software](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal-generating_software "Fractal-generating software")

*   [![Image 7: Fractal image generated by Electric Sheep](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Electricsheep-14525.jpg/330px-Electricsheep-14525.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Electricsheep-14525.jpg "Fractal image generated by Electric Sheep") Fractal image generated by [Electric Sheep](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_Sheep "Electric Sheep") 
*   [![Image 8: A Fibonacci word fractal](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/FWF_Samuel_Monnier_d%C3%A9tail.jpg/250px-FWF_Samuel_Monnier_d%C3%A9tail.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:FWF_Samuel_Monnier_d%C3%A9tail.jpg "A Fibonacci word fractal") A [Fibonacci word](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fibonacci_word "Fibonacci word") fractal

Fractals of all kinds have been used as the basis for digital art and animation. High resolution color graphics became increasingly available at scientific research labs in the mid-1980s. Scientific forms of art, including fractal art, have developed separately from mainstream culture.[[10]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-ciem-10) Starting with 2-dimensional details of fractals, such as the Mandelbrot Set, fractals have found artistic application in fields as varied as texture generation, plant growth simulation, and landscape generation.

Fractals are sometimes combined with [evolutionary algorithms](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolutionary_algorithms "Evolutionary algorithms"), either by iteratively choosing good-looking specimens in a set of random variations of a fractal artwork and producing new variations, to avoid dealing with cumbersome or unpredictable parameters, or collectively, as in the [Electric Sheep](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Electric_Sheep "Electric Sheep") project, where people use [fractal flames](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_flame "Fractal flame") rendered with [distributed computing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Distributed_computing "Distributed computing") as their [screensaver](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Screensaver "Screensaver") and "rate" the flame they are viewing, influencing the server, which reduces the traits of the undesirables, and increases those of the desirables to produce a computer-generated, community-created piece of art.

Many fractal images are admired because of their perceived [harmony](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Principles_of_art&action=edit&redlink=1 "Principles of art (page does not exist)"). This is typically achieved by the [patterns](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pattern "Pattern") which emerge from the [balance](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Principles_of_art&action=edit&redlink=1 "Principles of art (page does not exist)") of order and [chaos](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chaos_theory "Chaos theory"). Similar qualities have been described in [Chinese painting](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chinese_painting "Chinese painting") and [miniature trees and rockeries](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Penjing "Penjing").[[11]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-caftc-11)

Landscapes
----------

[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fractal_art&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Landscapes")]

Main article: [Fractal landscape](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_landscape "Fractal landscape")

[![Image 13](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Mandelbrot_island.jpg/250px-Mandelbrot_island.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:Mandelbrot_island.jpg)

A 3D landscape generated with [Terragen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Terragen "Terragen"), using the Mandelbrot set

The first fractal image that was intended to be a work of art was probably the famous one on the cover of _[Scientific American](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific\_American "Scientific American")_, August 1985. This image showed a [landscape](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Landscape "Landscape") formed from the potential function on the domain outside the (usual) [Mandelbrot set](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mandelbrot_set "Mandelbrot set"). However, as the potential function grows fast near the boundary of the Mandelbrot set, it was necessary for the creator to let the landscape grow downwards, so that it looked as if the Mandelbrot set was a [plateau](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Plateau "Plateau") atop a mountain with steep sides. The same technique was used a year after in some images in _[The Beauty of Fractals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The\_Beauty\_of\_Fractals "The Beauty of Fractals")_ by [Heinz-Otto Peitgen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heinz-Otto_Peitgen "Heinz-Otto Peitgen") and [Michael M. Richter](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_M._Richter "Michael M. Richter"). They provide a formula to estimate the distance from a point outside the Mandelbrot set to the boundary of the Mandelbrot set (and a similar formula for the Julia sets). Landscapes can, for example, be formed from the distance function for a family of iterations of the form z 2+a z 4+c{\displaystyle z^{2}+az^{4}+c}![Image 14: {\displaystyle z^{2}+az^{4}+c}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/29f6ce1a7cbc3df5604eedc12503d955f7d72050).

British artists include [William Latham](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/William_Latham_(computer_scientist) "William Latham (computer scientist)"), who has used fractal geometry and other computer graphics techniques in his works.[[12]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-fpc-12) and Vienna Forrester who creates flame fractal art using data extracted from her photographs. [Greg Sams](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Greg_Sams "Greg Sams") has used fractal designs in postcards, T-shirts, and textiles. American [Vicky Brago-Mitchell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Vicky_Brago-Mitchell "Vicky Brago-Mitchell") has created fractal art which has appeared in exhibitions and on magazine covers. [Carlos Ginzburg](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carlos_Ginzburg "Carlos Ginzburg") has explored fractal art and developed a concept called "homo fractalus" which is based around the idea that the human is the ultimate fractal.[[13]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-cagi-13) Merrin Parkers from New Zealand specialises in fractal art.[[14]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-hfn-14)[Kerry Mitchell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kerry_Mitchell "Kerry Mitchell") wrote a "Fractal Art Manifesto", claiming that.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-manifesto-15) In Italy, the artist Giorgio Orefice wrote the "Fractalism" manifesto, founding a Fractalism cultural mouvement in 1999.[[16]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-16)

> Fractal Art is a subclass of two-dimensional visual art, and is in many respects similar to photography—another art form that was greeted by skepticism upon its arrival. Fractal images typically are manifested as [prints](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Printmaking "Printmaking"), bringing fractal artists into the company of painters, photographers, and printmakers. Fractals exist natively as electronic images. This is a format that traditional visual artists are quickly embracing, bringing them into Fractal Art's digital realm. Generating fractals can be an artistic endeavor, a mathematical pursuit, or just a soothing diversion. However, Fractal Art is clearly distinguished from other digital activities by what it is, and by what it is not.[[15]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fractal_art#cite_note-manifesto-15)

According to Mitchell, fractal art is not computerized art, lacking in rules, unpredictable, nor something that any person with access to a computer can do well. Instead, fractal art is expressive, creative, and requires input, effort, and intelligence. Fractal Art.
============

Fractal arts are geometric designs reminiscent of recurring patterns in nature but devised by algorithms and equations. They can be captivating creative pieces, with many famous artists offering their own versions of fractal designs.

What are fractal patterns?
--------------------------

Fractal art pairs mathematic equations with recursive, equation-led [generative art](https://www.adobe.com/uk/products/firefly/features/ai-art-generator.html) to create an infinitesimal number of fractal patterns that hold an almost space-like, artistic quality that you can fall into. Simply put, a fractal is a repeating shape that gets smaller to then create abstract art and shapes.

The Mandelbrot set – a mathematical set (a collection of mathematical objects: numbers, shapes, variables) – gained popularity outside of mathematics for its complex structures and patterns arising from simple rules. These fractals were first discovered by mathematician Benoit Mandelbrot in 1980, though before the visuals, the set was devised by Robert W. Brooks and Peter Matelski in 1978.

Examples of fractal art.
------------------------

There are many fractal designs devised from repeating patterns – not only in digital art but in nature too. Think the swirls of coral and shells along the coastline, the symmetrical lattice of a snowflake and the geometric patterns of plants of all shapes. These are all natural fractals.

Fractal designs can also be seen in Roman architecture, though these may not be fractal artwork in the way we came to know them with the Mandelbrot set.

Inside the Mandelbrot fractal alone, there are a multitude of shapes and patterns as the set reduces. As you magnify, the simple set gives way to a valley of sea horses and double spirals, which in turn reveal curled tails, crowns, and satellites. It’s a psychedelic piece that consistently reduces into something more.

Other examples of fractal objects include the Menger sponge and the Sierpinski triangle. Additionally, some famous art prints – such as the poured patterns of American artists Jackson Pollock – are considered to be fractals.

In the modern era, abstract fractal art can be constructed through fractal art programmes and equations to generate shapes and patterns from sets. While it takes an artistic eye to identify patterns that resonate, you can generate them easily with little artistic or mathematical knowledge.

![Image 11](https://www.adobe.com/uk/creativecloud/illustration/discover/digital-art/media_1b271b6b97f2d02bd914f156209eb1c17ce46ea0d.png?width=750&format=png&optimize=medium)

How to make fractal art.
------------------------

While fractal art can be created using special programmes, you can also create them yourself without the need for anything fancy. Creating fractal art is more accessible than it was in 1980.

Since a fractal pertains to an image repeated in a shape, there are many ways to draw fractal designs following this basic premise:

*   In your chosen programme, draw a large version of a shape, pattern, or image that you want to repeat on itself.
*   Create a rule that you will repeat – stick to this rule. It may be to always add three triangles, for example.
*   Follow this rule again and again and again and… (repeat it, basically).
*   Continue until the details become undefined and you can no longer draw, or until you’re satisfied.

**How to create fractal art in Photoshop with brushes.**

Fractal art is an easy concept to grasp, and you could simply repeat the steps above in [Adobe Photoshop](https://www.adobe.com/uk/products/photoshop.html) to create your own fractal images. However, with a touch of ingenuity, you can make your own fractal images in a fraction of the time.

With Photoshop, you’ve got an array of options at your fingertips to create self-similar, never-ending patterns that tumble into fractal images. Photoshop brushes also offer a selection of thematic, fractal brushes ready to go. These could be geometric brushes, floral or even themed from animals, or something altogether more abstract.

To use brushes for fractal art:

*   **Get a Brush**. Visit the [Photoshop Brushes](https://www.adobe.com/uk/products/photoshop/photoshop-brushes.html) page to discover fractal brushes, perfect for creating fractal art.
*   **Reopen Photoshop & Install**. Once you’ve got your brush, drag the fractal brush you downloaded into the Presets/Brushes folder located in your Photoshop installation folder.
*   **Open a Document & Select your Brush**. Re-open Adobe and create a new document. Select the Brush tool from the Tools menu and click the drop-down next to the brush selection. Click the gear-cog and choose Load Brushes. Then, simply pick your brush and click Load.
*   **Choose Your Brush Size**. Once you’ve chosen your fractal art brush, you’re good to go – just amend the size using the size slider.
*   **Create**. Click on the canvas and you’ve begun. You can now start creating fractal art. Play about with the size, colour, and patterns to see what you can create.

**How to create fractal art in Photoshop from scratch.**

Want to create fractal art in Photoshop without using pre-made brushes? With Photoshop, you can remove some of the tedium of creating fractal images thanks to our useful Photoshop tools.

To get started with an example fractal:

*   **Open Photoshop**. To start, launch Photoshop and create a new document for your fractal art.
*   **Add a Ruler**. Next, click **View** and choose **Ruler** from the menu. With the Horizontal Ruler, drag your cursor toward the centre of the canvas. Then, do the same again with the **Vertical Ruler**. Now, you can determine the centre point of the canvas.
*   **Create a Background**. Grab your **Paint Bucket** or **Gradient** tools and create a background for your image, depending on what you prefer.
*   **Add References**. Draw a circle in the middle of the canvas using the **Ellipse** tool and the centre point as a guideline – you could use other shapes if you prefer.
*   **Layer Up**. From the **Layers** window, **Create a New Group** and name it “Fractal”, “Pattern”, or something similar. Drag the Ellipse layer into the new Fractal layer on the **Layers** menu.
*   **Select Blending Options**. On the menu bar, click **Layer** and then **Layer Styles**. On that menu, click **Blending Styles**. Customise and apply different effects such as gradient, shadow, embossing, and even texture.
*   **Duplicate the Circle Layer**. Use the alt key and drag the circle to duplicate the layer or make a copy. Press Ctrl + T while holding Shift to easily resize the circle by half, then create two more layers and place these in a pattern around your circle. This is the start of your fractal art, so place them as you see fit.
*   **Merge Layers**. Merge your original circle layer and the smaller circle layers in the **Layers** window. Simply right-click and then choose **Merge Layers**. Name this layer something to differentiate, like “Fractal 1” or “Pattern 1”, as you’ll be adding more as you continue.
*   **Transform and Duplicate**. Duplicate this layer you created by pressing Ctrl + Alt + T. This time, move the pattern off-centre (for this example, left). Then hold Shift and drag the free transform grid to size it and rotate it.
*   **Alter the Transform Centre**. Once again, press Ctrl + Alt + T to mimic the previous effect on a new Layer. Keep pressing Ctrl + Alt + T to create a fractal image with the pattern set you created. Right-click on the original “Fractal” group you made in the Layers window then click **Duplicate Group**. Look for the eye icon to hide the original “Fractal” group.
*   **Place Fractal Layer**. Click and drag the newest fractal layer to beneath and to the right of the images centre point. Then, rotate the copy (in this example, by 90 degrees) and press Ctrl + Shift + T.
*   **Experiment**. This example gives you the method, but you have creative freedom. Try new shapes, images, rotations, and placements to create your own, unique fractal images. You could even follow along while experimenting – give at a go and see how your piece of art turns out.

**Creating fractal art with Adobe Firefly**.

Generative AI tools, such as [Adobe Firefly](https://www.adobe.com/uk/products/firefly.html), can create incredible fractal art with unique prompts in a fraction of the time. As an artform which found its success in equations, it’s particularly suited to [AI image generation](https://www.adobe.com/uk/products/firefly/features/text-to-image.html) – and it couldn’t be easier. Just write out your prompt and watch AI fractal art images spiral to life.

*   **Adjust Your Results**. Once Firefly generates your images, you can amend the style, lighting, and effects. Finally, you’ll have four computer-generated fractal images to choose from. If one speaks to you more than the others but isn’t quite perfect, you can click it for more regenerations with that as a baseline.

Fractal art FAQs.
-----------------

**How do people make fractal art?**

There are a lot of ways to make fractal art, though the most common ways are to use generative AI to create fractal images, to use fractal generation software, or to create your own using Adobe Photoshop’s tools.

**Which famous artists used fractals?**

Popular artists that used fractal images in their work include Desmond Paul Henry, William Latham, and Hamid Naderi Yaganeh, though fractals can be seen in many classic artworks and forms of architecture. For example, Turbulence by Leonardo da Vinci could be said to use fractals, though not as we know them today.

**How do you know if something is a fractal?**

One of the defining features of a fractal is its self-similarity – a substructure must resemble its superstructure to be considered a fractal. For example, in the Mandlebrot set, the seahorse is a recurring image that you can see in other parts of the fractal.

Fractals have transformed modern art, inspiring artists like Jackson Pollock, who utilized fractal techniques to create dynamic compositions that echo nature's inherent patterns and complexities.

Fractal geometry informs cultural design across various traditions, from Islamic geometric patterns to Hindu temple architecture, showcasing the universal appeal and intricate beauty of repetitive structures.
